question: Define the Cartesian product of two sets A and B. Given the sets A = {1, 2} and B = {x, y}, list all elements of A × B and B × A, and explain the difference between the two resulting sets.
expectedPoints: Definition: A × B is the set of all ordered pairs (a, b) where a ∈ A and b ∈ B.
Calculation: A × B = {(1, x), (1, y), (2, x), (2, y)}.
Explanation: A × B ≠ B × A because the order of elements in an ordered pair matters; Cartesian product is not commutative.

question: Explain what a relation R from a set A to a set B is, using the definition involving Cartesian products. Then, for the sets A = {3, 4, 5} and B = {1, 2}, define a relation R = {(a, b) | a > b}. List the elements of R and represent this relation using an arrow diagram.
expectedPoints: Definition: A relation R from A to B is a subset of the Cartesian product A × B.
Identify the condition: The relation includes pairs where the element from A is greater than the element from B.
Arrow Diagram Description: Arrows should be drawn from each element in A (3,4,5) to both elements in B (1,2) to visually represent every possible pair.

question: A relation R is defined on the set of integers Z as R = {(a, b) | a² = b²}. Prove that R is an equivalence relation. In your answer, detail the three properties (reflexive, symmetric, transitive) and find the distinct equivalence classes generated by this relation.
expectedPoints: Reflexive: For any a ∈ Z, a² = a², so (a, a) ∈ R.
Symmetric: If (a, b) ∈ R, then a² = b², which implies b² = a², so (b, a) ∈ R.
Transitive: If (a, b) ∈ R and (b, c) ∈ R, then a² = b² and b² = c², so a² = c², meaning (a, c) ∈ R.
Equivalence Classes: The class of an integer a is [a] = { -a, a }. The distinct classes are sets of opposites, e.g., [0] = {0}, [1] = {-1, 1}, [2] = {-2, 2}, etc.
